text: Hello, I'm sharing a case of a robotic ultra-low anterior, essentially a coloanal, for a low T-turectal cancer. This was at about six centimeters from the anal verge. I have placed an ICG tattoo there because it was a small lesion and did not expect it to be visible from the outside. as well as the fact that I usually do partial TMEs, tumor-specific mesorectal excisions and not always a total. So if I could preserve a little rectum, that's what I was gonna do.

I'm highlighting in this case just the steps of TME or the process of a TME and not necessarily all the steps of the case. I did forget to turn the recording back on after I had placed my anvil so you don't see the anastomosis, but I can assure you that for a stapled anastomosis, I guess it was as good as you could get. I will do stapled anastomosis when there is nothing that I can sew to. And I would prefer to do a stapled anastomosis when possible in a local anal compared to a sewn coloanal.
The surgeon cones in on the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and performs a high ligation. A window is created around the artery, and the vessel is sealed using a vessel sealer device.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection, focusing on identifying the left-sided plane to gain a clear view around the mesorectum. The dissection progresses down the upper part of the left side of the total mesorectal excision (TME).

The surgeon marks the planned line of dissection and performs posterior dissection, staying within the defined anatomical plane. The right lateral dissection follows, with the ICG tattoo visible as a reference point.

The surgeon performs total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection using monopolar cautery, carefully identifying anatomical planes. The dissection progresses from the right lateral to anterior and similarly on the left lateral side, facilitated by the wider pelvis of the female patient.

The surgeon enters the rectovaginal septum and carefully dissects to completely remove the mesorectum while preserving the vaginal structure. The uterus is suspended to the abdominal wall with a Keith needle to enhance exposure for the deeper dissection.

The surgeon works circumferentially around the mesorectum, aiming to dissect two to three centimeters beneath the visible ICG tattoos. The surgical field shows the use of multiple instruments, with the camera positioned in port three, utilizing two instruments on the left and one on the right.
The surgeon continues circumferential dissection around the mesorectum, carefully maintaining an even plane without creating holes in the tissue.

The surgeon carefully dissects below the ICG tattoos placed at the tumor level, revealing the levator muscles beneath. The mesorectum is lifted away from the levator muscles, maintaining clear separation between these anatomical structures.

The surgeon operates with four ports, switching the camera to arm number two for a straight view into the deep pelvis. Arm number three, equipped with a twelve millimeter trocar, is prepared for stapling. The specimen is extracted through the ileostomy site, minimizing the risk of hernia at the incision.

The surgeon uses the camera in arm number two to focus on detaching the levator muscles from their attachments to the mesorectum, ensuring the dissection is below the tumor level. The surgeon's finger and scope are used to gain additional space, preparing for a coloanal anastomosis. The entire mesorectum is being taken, with plans to staple flush with the levator muscles.

The surgeon introduces a 45mm stapler, positioning it from anterior to posterior. They carefully check to ensure the vagina is not inadvertently included before proceeding with another staple to complete the transection.

The surgeon demonstrates the dissection of the proximal mesentery, approaching the cephalad aspect of the inferior mesenteric artery. The chosen proximal margin is in the proximal sigmoid colon, with visible redundancy in the sigmoid and descending colon, indicating no tension in the tissue.

The surgeon marks the planned margin with cautery to prepare for exteriorization and placement of an anvil, as the location is too low for suturing from the nominal side. The dissected plane is visible, with the transected rectum located in the crease of the levator muscles.
